title: Hardcoded CICS username and password in SagaiMain.callSagai()
description: Line 102 of SagaiMain.java passes the literal username 'MZPESY01' and password 'NOPASS' directly into ECIConnectionSpec. These credentials are committed to source control and will appear in every build artifact. Any developer, CI system, or code-leakage incident exposes live CICS access credentials. The static analyser flagged API_KEY in Constants.java but missed this separate credential pair buried in the SAGAI integration class.

title: TLS certificate validation disabled across all HTTP client construction sites
description: AbstractService.getHttpClient() (line 707), AbstractResource, SagaiContractServiceImpl (line 275), SagaiWarrantyServiceImpl (line 816), SagaiMain (line 289), and SagaiServiceCall (line 145) all build an SSLContext with TrustStrategy = (certificate, authType) -> true and attach NoopHostnameVerifier.INSTANCE. This disables both certificate chain validation and hostname matching, making every outbound HTTPS call to Corvet, ESA, and SAGAI services trivially vulnerable to man-in-the-middle interception. The pattern appears in at least six classes.

title: VIN numbers and licence-plate numbers logged in plain text at INFO level
description: VehicleIdentificationNumberResource.getVins() logs the full VIN number, VIS number, and licence plate number at INFO level (around line 185) on every request with 20+ parameters. VIN numbers are a vehicle-linked personal identifier regulated under GDPR in EU markets. These log entries will be stored in centralised log aggregators, accessible by support staff and audit tools, and will appear in server access logs and APM traces. A data-breach or improper log retention would constitute a GDPR violation.
beforeCode: logger.info("Calling VIN Service (/vins) ... vin = {}, vis = {}, licensePlateNumber = {}, idToCheck = {} ", ..., vinNumber, visNumber, licencePlateNo, idToCheck);
afterCode:
// Mask PII: log only the first 3 chars of VIN and omit licence plate
logger.info("Calling VIN Service (/vins) ... vin = {}***, source = {}, country = {}", vinNumber != null ? vinNumber.substring(0,3) : "null", parameter.getSource(), parameter.getCountry());

title: Live API key stored inside VinRequestParameter — risks serialisation and logging exposure
description: VehicleIdentificationNumberResource.getVins() calls parameter.setApiKey(apiKey) (line 162), storing the caller's API key inside the domain parameter object. VinRequestParameter is passed through multiple service layers and logged in various places. If the object is ever serialised to JSON (e.g., as a cache entry or audit log), the raw API key will be exposed. API keys should be validated at the boundary and never carried deeper into the application.
afterCode:
// Do not store the raw key in the parameter object; consumer name is sufficient
// parameter.setApiKey(apiKey);  // REMOVE

title: No rate limiting on GET /vins — enables enumeration and credential-brute-force
description: GET /vins and GET /activeContractsDAI accept an API key in a header and trigger expensive downstream calls to CICS, Corvet, and SAGAI with no request throttling, lockout, or per-consumer quota enforcement. An attacker with a leaked API key can enumerate vehicle data at full network speed. The API key validation in AbstractResource returns 401 on an invalid key but imposes no delay or lockout after repeated failures, enabling key-guessing attacks against any known consumer key prefix.

title: getSingleResult() called twice on the same query in getCountryConfigValue()
description: SagaiDeployedRepositoryImpl.getCountryConfigValue() calls q.getSingleResult() first as a null guard (line 79) and then a second time to assign the result (line 80). This executes two separate database round-trips where one suffices. If the row is modified or deleted between the two calls the method may log the correct value but return a stale or different row. The JPA spec also states that getSingleResult() throws NonUniqueResultException if multiple results exist, so the first call can throw without the second ever running.

title: getResultList() called TWICE per invocation in byVehicleIdentificationNumber()
description: VehicleIdentificationNumberJpaRepository.byVehicleIdentificationNumber() (lines 109-110) calls query.getResultList() for the isEmpty() guard and then calls it again to retrieve the first element. Every VIN lookup on the hot GET /vins path therefore executes the same query twice against the database. Under the SLOW 888ms p50 observed on /vins, halving primary-key lookups will materially reduce load.

title: getResultList() called three times in getMarketCode() with misplaced null guard
description: SagaiDeployedRepositoryImpl.getMarketCode() (line 148) executes: `if (!query.getResultList().isEmpty() && null != query.getResultList() && !query.getResultList().contains(null))`. This fires three separate DB queries per invocation. The null guard `null != query.getResultList()` is also placed after isEmpty() — it cannot protect against null since getResultList() per JPA spec returns an empty list, never null. On the GET /vinscontracts path, this method is called multiple times, compounding the overhead.

title: insertVinData() and insertWarranty() have no @Transactional boundary
description: AbstractService.insertVinData() (line 126) calls vinRepository.insertVehicleIdentificationNumberInfoInCin() and AbstractService.insertWarranty() calls vinWarrantyRepository.inserVinWarranty() with no outer @Transactional annotation. Individual callers in CartellServiceImpl, CorvetServiceImpl, etc. are @Transactional at the class level but when insertVinData and insertWarranty are called in sequence from the overriding service methods, a runtime failure between the two writes leaves a VIN row without a warranty row — a half-committed state that causes inconsistent downstream responses.
beforeCode: public void insertVinData(String vinNumber, JsonObject objectFromString, Timestamp timestamp, String source, String lanuageCode, String country) {
afterCode:
@Transactional
public void insertVinData(String vinNumber, JsonObject objectFromString, Timestamp timestamp, String source, String lanuageCode, String country) {

title: New CloseableHttpClient built on every HTTP call — no connection pooling
description: AbstractService.getHttpClient() (line 706) constructs a brand new CloseableHttpClient (including SSLContext) on every invocation. Because every upstream call (Corvet, ESA, Cartell, SAGAI REST) goes through this factory, /vins generates 2-6 new TCP+TLS connections per request. With p50 already at 888ms on /vins this accounts for a significant fraction of latency: each TLS handshake costs 50-200ms. The correct pattern is a singleton PoolingHttpClientConnectionManager with configured maxTotal and defaultMaxPerRoute.

title: validateRequestHeaderParameters() does not short-circuit — later error overwrites earlier one
description: AbstractResource.validateRequestHeaderParameters() (line 177) first sets `response = validateApiKey(request)`, then unconditionally evaluates country validity and language validity, each potentially overwriting the response variable. If the API key is missing (a security violation), the method may return 'Non existing Language' instead of 'API-Key is not present in Header'. This masks the real rejection reason and could be exploited to enumerate valid countries/languages without a valid key.

title: Exception in getActiveContracts() swallowed — caller receives 200 OK with empty response
description: ActiveContractsServiceImpl.getActiveContracts() (line 52-56) wraps `callDAIRecursively()` in a try-catch that logs the error but then continues. The method proceeds to build a response from an empty `DaiResponse` object, returning HTTP 200 with a null `returnCode` field. Callers cannot distinguish a successful empty contract list from a total service failure. This silently hides DAI integration outages and produces data consistency issues for consumers who treat 200 as authoritative.

title: MDC correlation ID not propagated into CICS callSagai() — CICS errors untraceable
description: VehicleIdentificationNumberResource sets MDC.put("REQUEST_ID", correlationId) at the start of each request, but SagaiMain.callSagai() (line 79) does not read or log this value. CICS-level errors and timeouts are logged by SagaiMain with only `functionName` and `country`, making it impossible to correlate a CICS failure with the originating HTTP request in a log aggregation system. Under the 303ms p50 response on /health/daiContractsHealth, CICS interactions need traceability.
beforeCode: logger.info("Calling sagai for functionName: {}, country: {}, transactionName:{}", functionName, abstractBean.getCodePays(), transactionName);
afterCode:
String requestId = MDC.get("REQUEST_ID");
logger.info("Calling sagai for functionName: {}, country: {}, transactionName:{}, requestId:{}", functionName, abstractBean.getCodePays(), transactionName, requestId);

title: All repository read methods missing @Transactional(readOnly=true)
description: Methods like SagaiDeployedRepositoryImpl.getCountryConfigValue(), getSagaiCountryID(), getMarketCode(), VehicleIdentificationNumberJpaRepository.byVehicleIdentificationNumber(), and all equivalent read-only finders across 8 repository impl classes do not declare @Transactional(readOnly=true). Without this, Hibernate performs dirty-checking and flushing on every read, increasing transaction overhead. readOnly=true also enables optional per-provider optimisations (e.g., routing to a read replica). Given that /vins is already SLOW at 888ms p50, this is a realistic latency reduction.
